items.front: If the route beyond a clearance limit differs from the last routing cleared, what must be issued?
items.back: The route the pilot can expect beyond the clearance limit.

items.front: When may holding instructions be eliminated in a clearance to a holding fix?
items.back: When the pilot is informed that no delay is expected.

items.front: When may most holding instructions be omitted for a charted pattern already in the assigned procedure or route?
items.back: When the charted holding direction and the words "as published" are still stated, unless the pilot requests complete holding instructions.

items.front: When is the EFC item omitted?
items.back: When no delay is expected.

items.front: When delay is expected, when should the clearance-limit, holding, and EFC items normally be issued?
items.back: At least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit, or immediately if it is already less than 5 minutes from the fix.

items.front: What phraseology is used when no delay is expected at the fix?
items.back: Cleared to (fix), no delay expected.

items.front: What phraseology is used when onward routing beyond the clearance limit must be stated later?
items.back: Expect further clearance via (routing).

items.front: What phraseology is used when an accurate delay estimate cannot immediately be determined?
items.back: Delay indefinite, (reason if known), expect further clearance (time).

items.front: If no delay is expected, when should a clearance beyond the clearance limit be issued?
items.back: As soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

items.front: What three items are included when issuing a clearance beyond a clearance limit?
items.back: The new clearance limit or approach clearance, the route of flight, and the assigned altitude if it differs from the present altitude.

items.front: When may the phrase "via last routing cleared" be used?
items.back: Only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit remains valid and verbiage will be reduced.

items.front: If no holding pattern is charted and none was issued, what should pilots do before reaching the fix?
items.back: Ask ATC for holding instructions prior to reaching the fix.

items.front: If a pilot cannot obtain holding instructions before reaching an uncharted fix, what hold is expected?
items.back: A standard pattern on the course on which the aircraft approached the fix, while requesting further clearance as soon as possible.

items.front: What should you do as soon as possible when you delay or expect to delay aircraft?
items.back: Advise your supervisor or flow controller.

items.front: When arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, when must the responsible center issue total delay information?
items.back: As soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area, and whenever possible by the first center controller to communicate with the aircraft.

items.front: When tower en route control service is being provided, which facility issues total delay information after the aircraft enters its area?
items.back: The approach control facility whose area contains the destination airport.

items.front: When may the 30-minute delay advisories be omitted?
items.back: When total delay information is available via ATIS and the pilot has not requested delay information.

items.front: What phraseology is used for total arrival delay information?
items.back: (Airport) arrival delays (time in minutes or hours).

items.front: What direction information must a holding clearance include?
items.back: The direction of holding from the fix or waypoint.

items.front: When may the holding fix be omitted from the holding-instruction element?
items.back: When it was already included at the beginning of the transmission as the clearance limit.

items.front: How is leg length specified when DME or RNAV is used?
items.back: In miles, unless the pilot requests minutes or the controller considers minutes necessary.

items.front: When must turn direction be stated in holding instructions?
items.back: When left turns are to be made, when the pilot requests it, or when the controller considers it necessary.

items.front: When is a maximum holding airspeed advisory required?
items.back: When the pattern protects a higher approved speed, when the aircraft is observed deviating from holding airspace, or when the airspeed-restricted holding icon has not been published.

items.front: What phraseology states the protected holding speed?
items.back: Maximum holding airspeed is (speed) knots.

items.front: What phraseology begins a standard holding instruction?
items.back: Hold (direction) of (fix or waypoint) on (specified radial, course, bearing, track, airway, azimuth, or route).

items.front: When may a visual holding point be used?
items.back: When the pilot can determine it by visual reference to the surface and is familiar with it.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a visual holding point?
items.back: Hold at (location) until (time or other condition).

items.front: What if the pilot is not familiar with the proposed visual holding point?
items.back: It may not be used.

items.front: What two conditions must both exist before a visual holding point is acceptable?
items.back: The pilot must be able to determine it by visual reference to the surface and must be familiar with it.

items.front: When may a pilot's requested deviation from the prescribed holding flight path be approved?
items.back: When obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

items.front: What two factors control approval of a holding flight-path deviation?
items.back: Obstacles and traffic conditions.

items.front: If traffic conditions do not permit the requested holding deviation, what is the correct action?
items.back: Do not approve the deviation.

items.front: Does pilot request alone justify a holding flight-path deviation?
items.back: No. Obstacles and traffic conditions must also permit it.

items.front: How is protection defined for an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Separate it from any aircraft occupying the course it would follow if it stopped receiving the NAVAID signal.

items.front: What traffic matters when protecting an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Traffic occupying the course the holding aircraft would follow after signal loss.

items.front: Why is extra protection required at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Because the aircraft may continue along a predictable course if it loses the NAVAID signal while holding.

items.front: What if another aircraft is about to enter that protected signal-loss course?
items.back: Provide separation so the two aircraft do not occupy that course together.

items.front: What weather condition triggers the ILS holding restriction?
items.back: An official ceiling below 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles.

items.front: What holding position is prohibited once the ILS protection trigger exists?
items.back: Holding below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the OM or substitute fix and the airport.

items.front: What additional limitation appears in the USAF note?
items.back: The holding restriction applies only when an arriving aircraft is between the OM or substitute fix and the runway.

items.front: What distance from the localizer is part of the protected area?
items.back: On or within 1 statute mile of the localizer.

items.front: What altitude floor is part of the prohibited hold area once the trigger exists?
items.back: Below 5,000 feet AGL.
